abstract: We formulate long-context language modeling as a problem in continual learning rather than architecture design. Under this formulation, we only use a standard architecture -- a Transformer with sliding-window attention. However, our model continues learning at test time via next-token prediction on the given context, compressing the context it reads into its weights. In addition, we improve the model's initialization for learning at test time via meta-learning at training time. Overall, our method, a form of Test-Time Training (TTT), is End-to-End (E2E) both at test time (via next-token prediction) and training time (via meta-learning), in contrast to previous forms. We conduct extensive experiments with a focus on scaling properties. In particular, for 3B models trained with 164B tokens, our method (TTT-E2E) scales with context length in the same way as Transformer with full attention, while others, such as Mamba 2 and Gated DeltaNet, do not. However, similar to RNNs, TTT-E2E has constant inference latency regardless of context length, making it 2.7 times faster than full attention for 128K context. Our code is publicly available.

keyInsights: Long-context processing can be effectively achieved by treating it as a continual learning task, allowing models to continuously adapt and compress information from the input stream into their weights.

An 'End-to-End' approach, where both test-time adaptation and training-time meta-learning directly optimize for the next-token prediction objective, is critical for bridging the performance-efficiency gap.

Combining local, efficient memory (Sliding-Window Attention) with dynamic, global context compression into model weights offers a robust mechanism for scaling performance with context length at a constant inference cost.
originalProblem: Full-attention Transformers incur quadratic computational cost (O(T^2)) with context length, making them impractical for very long sequences exceeding tens of thousands of tokens.

Existing efficient models like Sliding-Window Attention or RNNs offer linear computational cost but typically compromise on performance and struggle to leverage truly global context effectively at extreme lengths.
Prior Test-Time Training (TTT) methods for long contexts often suffered from a mismatch between training and test-time objectives and were not fully 'end-to-end' in their adaptation mechanism.
results: TTT-E2E demonstrates performance scaling with context length similar to full attention, achieving lower losses than full attention up to 128K context while maintaining constant inference latency, leading to a 2.7x speedup over full attention at 128K context.
Ablations show that effective TTT-E2E requires frequent, small updates (smaller mini-batch sizes) and sufficient trainable capacity (e.g., updating 1/4 of the model's blocks) for context compression.

While TTT-E2E consistently yields lower losses across various context lengths and improves long decoding sequence quality, it performs significantly worse than full attention on 'Needle in a Haystack' tasks, suggesting a trade-off between compression/generalization and precise, lossless recall.
solution: TTT-E2E reframes long-context language modeling as a continual learning problem, building upon a Transformer architecture equipped with Sliding-Window Attention (SWA) for local context.

During test-time inference, the model continuously adapts by taking gradient steps on the next-token prediction loss for incoming mini-batches, compressing long-range context into its MLP weights as 'long-term memory'.
At training time, meta-learning (an outer loop) optimizes the model's initial weights to ensure that they are optimally prepared for subsequent rapid adaptation via TTT at test time.
paper_summary.summary: End-to-End Test-Time Training (TTT-E2E) enables language models to process extremely long contexts with performance comparable to full attention Transformers, while maintaining the constant inference latency of recurrent models. This approach achieves a 2.7x speedup over full attention at 128K context on H100 GPUs, effectively transforming the efficient but limited Sliding-Window Attention baseline into the best-performing method for extended sequences.

title: End-to-End Test-Time Training for Long Context

abstract: Test-time training (TTT) with KV binding as sequence modeling layer is commonly interpreted as a form of online meta-learning that memorizes a key-value mapping at test time. However, our analysis reveals multiple phenomena that contradict this memorization-based interpretation. Motivated by these findings, we revisit the formulation of TTT and show that a broad class of TTT architectures can be expressed as a form of learned linear attention operator. Beyond explaining previously puzzling model behaviors, this perspective yields multiple practical benefits: it enables principled architectural simplifications, admits fully parallel formulations that preserve performance while improving efficiency, and provides a systematic reduction of diverse TTT variants to a standard linear attention form. Overall, our results reframe TTT not as test-time memorization, but as learned linear attention with enhanced representational capacity.

keyInsights: Test-Time Training with Key-Value binding (TTT-KVB) fundamentally operates as a learned linear attention operator, rather than a test-time memorization process.

This linear attention perspective provides natural explanations for previously paradoxical behaviors, such as the inverse relationship between inner-loop loss and performance, and the efficacy of gradient ascent.
Many complex components in existing TTT architectures, previously justified by the memorization hypothesis, are redundant or offer only marginal benefits.
originalProblem: The prevailing interpretation of Test-Time Training with Key-Value binding (TTT-KVB) as an "online meta-learning" or "memorization" process was inconsistent with empirical observations.
A "Memorization Paradox" existed, where improving the inner-loop loss (interpreted as stronger memorization) often degraded downstream task performance.
Complex TTT architectures lacked a unified theoretical framework, leading to increasingly intricate designs without clear justification for all components.
results: Empirically demonstrated that increasing inner-loop optimization degraded task performance, and replacing gradient descent with gradient ascent maintained or improved performance, refuting the memorization paradigm.
Achieved up to a 4.0x inference throughput speedup on the TTT layer and a 1.19x end-to-end training speedup for specific TTT models by implementing a parallel formulation.

Ablation studies revealed that restricting inner-loop updates to only the last layer parameters provided comparable or better performance, while removing components like weight normalization and complex MLPs caused only minor performance degradation, simplifying architectures.
solution: Conducted empirical experiments to highlight contradictions with the memorization paradigm, such as the effectiveness of gradient ascent and the insensitivity to query replacement.

Developed analytical derivations to prove that a broad class of TTT architectures, including those with multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) and momentum-based optimizers, can be reformulated as a learned linear attention operator.
Validated the practical implications through systematic ablation studies to simplify TTT models and derived fully parallel formulations for efficient inference and training.
paper_summary.summary: The paper re-interprets Test-Time Training with Key-Value binding (TTT-KVB), showing it is mathematically equivalent to a learned linear attention mechanism, which allows for substantial efficiency improvements and simplified model architectures. Researchers provided analytical proofs demonstrating this equivalence, even for complex TTT variants, resolving empirical paradoxes.

title: Test-Time Training with KV Binding Is Secretly Linear Attention

abstract: We formulate long-context language modeling as a problem in continual learning rather than architecture design. Under this formulation, we only use a standard architecture -- a Transformer with sliding-window attention. However, our model continues learning at test time via next-token prediction on the given context, compressing the context it reads into its weights. In addition, we improve the model's initialization for learning at test time via meta-learning at training time. Overall, our method, a form of Test-Time Training (TTT), is End-to-End (E2E) both at test time (via next-token prediction) and training time (via meta-learning), in contrast to previous forms. We conduct extensive experiments with a focus on scaling properties. In particular, for 3B models trained with 164B tokens, our method (TTT-E2E) scales with context length in the same way as Transformer with full attention, while others, such as Mamba 2 and Gated DeltaNet, do not. However, similar to RNNs, TTT-E2E has constant inference latency regardless of context length, making it 2.7 times faster than full attention for 128K context. Our code is publicly available.

keyInsights: Long-context language modeling can be reframed as a continual learning problem, where the model dynamically compresses context into its weights during inference, rather than solely relying on architectural design for memory.

Optimizing the model's learnability at test time through meta-learning (end-to-end training) resolves the mismatch of prior TTT approaches, aligning the training objective with the actual test-time adaptation process.

The compression mechanism in TTT-E2E, while effective for general language modeling, differs from the nearly lossless recall of full attention, indicating a trade-off between efficient context utilization and explicit memorization of specific details.
originalProblem: Transformer models with full attention incur a quadratic computational cost with increasing context length, making them prohibitively expensive for very long sequences (e.g., 128K tokens).

Existing efficient long-context methods, such as RNNs (Mamba 2) or approximate attention (Sliding-Window Attention), offer constant inference cost but show reduced effectiveness or performance degradation as context length grows.

Previous Test-Time Training (TTT) methods typically involved layer-wise reconstruction losses or specific architectural modifications, lacking an end-to-end optimization for the final language modeling task.
results: TTT-E2E demonstrates scaling behavior in loss (perplexity) with context length similar to a Transformer with full attention, consistently outperforming other efficient baselines (SWA, Mamba 2) at longer contexts.
The method maintains constant inference latency per token regardless of context length, achieving a 2.7x speedup over full attention for a 128K context on an H100 GPU.

While effective for language modeling, TTT-E2E is significantly outperformed by full attention on "Needle in a Haystack" tasks, suggesting its compression mechanism is less suited for tasks requiring explicit, 'lossless' recall of specific details from long contexts.
solution: The TTT-E2E method augments a standard Transformer with Sliding-Window Attention (SWA) by continuously updating its MLP layer weights at test time using mini-batch gradient descent on the standard next-token prediction loss.

An outer meta-learning loop optimizes the model's initial weights (`W_0`) by computing "gradients of gradients" to ensure `W_0` is a good starting point for the subsequent test-time adaptation, making the entire process end-to-end.
Only the MLP layers in the last 1/4 of Transformer blocks are updated, and a second static MLP layer is added to updated blocks to mitigate catastrophic forgetting and store pre-trained knowledge.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Astera Institute, NVIDIA, Stanford, UC Berkeley, and UC San Diego developed TTT-E2E, a method that combines a standard Transformer with sliding-window attention and test-time learning to achieve constant inference cost while scaling performance (perplexity) with context length similarly to full attention.

abstract: We formulate long-context language modeling as a problem in continual learning rather than architecture design. Under this formulation, we only use a standard architecture -- a Transformer with sliding-window attention. However, our model continues learning at test time via next-token prediction on the given context, compressing the context it reads into its weights. In addition, we improve the model's initialization for learning at test time via meta-learning at training time. Overall, our method, a form of Test-Time Training (TTT), is End-to-End (E2E) both at test time (via next-token prediction) and training time (via meta-learning), in contrast to previous forms. We conduct extensive experiments with a focus on scaling properties. In particular, for 3B models trained with 164B tokens, our method (TTT-E2E) scales with context length in the same way as Transformer with full attention, while others, such as Mamba 2 and Gated DeltaNet, do not. However, similar to RNNs, TTT-E2E has constant inference latency regardless of context length, making it 2.7 times faster than full attention for 128K context. Our code is publicly available.

abstract: Test-time training (TTT) with KV binding as sequence modeling layer is commonly interpreted as a form of online meta-learning that memorizes a key-value mapping at test time. However, our analysis reveals multiple phenomena that contradict this memorization-based interpretation. Motivated by these findings, we revisit the formulation of TTT and show that a broad class of TTT architectures can be expressed as a form of learned linear attention operator. Beyond explaining previously puzzling model behaviors, this perspective yields multiple practical benefits: it enables principled architectural simplifications, admits fully parallel formulations that preserve performance while improving efficiency, and provides a systematic reduction of diverse TTT variants to a standard linear attention form. Overall, our results reframe TTT not as test-time memorization, but as learned linear attention with enhanced representational capacity.
